coredata.dc:title: The numerical and experimental investigation of the transient behaviours of a lithium-ion pouch battery cell under dynamic conditions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Tahirağaoğlu, Berkay
Sevilgen, Gökhan
Hamut, Halil Sadettin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the transient model of a high energy density Lithium-ion pouch battery cell is developed under dynamic conditions. The battery cell has a capacity of 73 Ah and volumetric energy density of 642 Wh L−1. This is one of the few studies included the electro-thermal behaviour of high energy density battery under transient conditions by using second ordered model. The transient model indicated that the state of charge (SOC) decreased by 7 % at the end of the WLTP driving cycle and this value is good agreement with the experimental data. Moreover, the developed model provides an accurate prediction of the terminal voltage with a R2 value of 0.99 and a maximum relative error of 2.0 %. Furthermore, the proposed model predicts the electro-thermal characteristics more precisely and the heat generation rate and the entropic term can also be determined without using measurement device. The calculated total heat emitted from battery is about 6W at 1 C-rate for constant current and the heat generation rate is a peak value of ±1.75 105 Wm−3 under dynamic conditions. By using the estimated heat generation rate, more effective cold plates will be designed for thermal management of high energy density battery cells.

head.ce:title: The numerical and experimental investigation of the transient behaviours of a lithium-ion pouch battery cell under dynamic conditions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the transient model of a high energy density Lithium-ion pouch battery cell is developed under dynamic conditions. The battery cell has a capacity of 73 Ah and volumetric energy density of 642 Wh L−1. This is one of the few studies included the electro-thermal behaviour of high energy density battery under transient conditions by using second ordered model. The transient model indicated that the state of charge (SOC) decreased by 7 % at the end of the WLTP driving cycle and this value is good agreement with the experimental data. Moreover, the developed model provides an accurate prediction of the terminal voltage with a R2 value of 0.99 and a maximum relative error of 2.0 %. Furthermore, the proposed model predicts the electro-thermal characteristics more precisely and the heat generation rate and the entropic term can also be determined without using measurement device. The calculated total heat emitted from battery is about 6W at 1 C-rate for constant current and the heat generation rate is a peak value of ±1.75 105 Wm−3 under dynamic conditions. By using the estimated heat generation rate, more effective cold plates will be designed for thermal management of high energy density battery cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Vehicles with conventional internal combustion engines threaten the health of the world due to fossil fuel consumption and harmful emissions to the environment [1–3]. Therefore, alternative vehicles such as electric, hydrogen and hybrid vehicle technologies are accepted as promising technologies [4,5]. Moreover, a wide range of alternative vehicles which have mechanical, hybrid, electro-chemical, chemical, electrical or thermal energy storage systems offered to the consumers [6,7]. The vehicles included lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) that are in electro-chemical category are the most used unit in terms of weight reduction and also high energy density [8–10]. Based on the developments over the energy storage units there are satisfactory advances on range capability of the electric vehicles (EVs), however, recent researches focused on improving fast charging capability and the challenges it brings [11–13]. Therefore, the controlling the thermal and electrical behaviour of the battery cells under dynamic conditions have become important due to thermal management of challenges under extreme weather conditions [14–16]. On the other hand, the battery cells have to operate with acceptable range for providing driver safety and also getting optimum performance [17–20]. The most used battery pack has battery modules included cells are located in the pack by using structural and electronic elements such as cover, frame and battery management system (BMS) [21–23]. The BMS unit have sub-components such as battery thermal management system (BTMS) that controlled the desirable temperature level of battery modules and also cells [24,25]. Possible safety risks due to temperature increase are eliminated by BTMS [26–30]. Therefore, the battery cell characteristics should be considered in terms of thermal and electrical properties under dynamic conditions.

There are various test procedures to investigate the battery characteristics. Among them, Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization (HPPC) and constant current (CC) charge-discharge test methods are commonly used in the current literature [31–36]. By performing these procedures, an equivalent circuit model is developed to understand the electrical behaviour of the battery cell. The electrical characteristics of the battery cell can be represented by first, second and third ordered equivalent circuit models, in general [37–43]. In these models, the cell consists of a resistor and different numbers of RC elements connected in series [44]. The resistor element represents ohmic resistance while the series-connected RC circuits explain fast and slow dynamics [45]. However, the electrical resistances of the cells are significantly affected by the temperature [46–49]. Therefore, the method has to be used with various temperatures to define the electrical parameters for different ambient conditions. In available literature, the most researches focused on the first ordered models, but for getting accurate results, the second and the third one should be used in the mathematical models [50]. In this paper, the second ordered model which represents electrical properties more precisely than the low ordered models was selected by using Worldwide Harmonized Light-Duty Vehicles Test Procedure (WLTP) driving conditions.

In this study, the 1D mathematical model included three sub-models named as (i) electric vehicle dynamic model (ii) battery pack thermal and (iii) electrical model. In order to design the electric vehicle dynamic model, it is necessary to simulate the transient behaviour of the battery pack by using WLTP driving cycle and other cycles such as New European Driving Cycle (NEDC) and Highway Fuel Economy Test Cycle (HWFET), etc. [51–56]. Among these cycles, WLTP has a velocity profile represents the vehicle at four different velocity zone and it is possible to obtain results similar to those of real road conditions [57]. Thus, in the vehicle dynamic model, the developed model was considered by using a generic vehicle which properties can be changed in terms of number of modules, electrical connectivity of battery pack, battery cell type and also driving conditions. The transient model of an electric vehicle is affected by environmental factors, vehicle characteristics, road conditions and driving profile [58]. In order to analyse the battery parameters of a vehicle in this cycle, it is necessary to determine the power demanded by the electric motors depending on the vehicle characteristics. Then, based on this battery configuration, the current and voltage demanded from the battery modules and also cells can be calculated.

In the thermal model of the battery pack, the results of a finite difference analysis and experimental data were combined to predict the thermal properties such as heat generation rate of battery cell under transient conditions. The electrical model developed in this study was utilized to simulate the State of Charge (SOC), current and voltage. The electrical and thermal models need to obtain their outputs so it can be said that these models were interrelated. Consequently, in the developed transient model, experimental, thermal and electrical results were combined to represent the EV for WLTP driving cycle.

With the consideration given above, the novelty of the present research and the main contributions to the scientific literature of this research can be stated as follows; an innovative approximate heat generation algorithm was developed for all cell types and for vehicles with different battery electrical configurations. There are few studies in the current literature on the electro-thermal behaviour of the battery under transient conditions by using second ordered equivalent circuit model. Another novelty of this study is that the battery cell used in this study had high energy density and also capacity which was used in EVs. From the results of the numerical data we can easily say that the calculated heat generation rate, SOC and voltage values were good agreement with the experimental data and also data mentioned in the available literature. As a result, by using the detailed electro-thermal model of an electric vehicle under the WLTP driving cycle, the thermal and electrical parameters of the battery cell during driving can be predicted precisely. Finite difference method (FDM) can be also used to calculate the heat generation and entropic term without using measurement device such as calorimeter and by using transient model developed in this study, the module and battery pack characteristics can also be evaluated. Thus, researchers can easily design innovative cold plates for the thermal management of pouch Li-Ion battery cells and also battery pack in the next stage by using heat generation rate and temperature distribution.

The transient model of the selected battery cell was investigated and this model included three different sub models named as (i) electric vehicle dynamic, (ii) electrical and (iii) thermal model. In general, the battery pack has modules included battery cells and a sample battery-cell module includes housing elements such as plates made of aluminium elements, thermal insulation layer (TIM), compression foam and also electrical elements. In the developed model, all these elements were eliminated that the battery cell was assumed as a single energy storage unit which was only electrically connected with the other cell. This simplification of mathematical model provided advantages in terms of calculation time and also shows the ideal situation that can be compared for the real conditions. In this paper, Matlab/Simulink software was used to develop the transient model, and the behaviour of the cell under dynamic conditions was determined through the embedding of data collected and battery characteristics obtained from experimental studies. In the transient model, firstly, power required from battery is calculated as pack level by using electrothermal model and then electrical parameters (e.g. current, terminal voltage, open circuit voltage, SOC and generated heat) were defined as cell level based on pack and module configurations. The methods used in this study were described in detail as followings.

In this study, to compose a transient model and investigate the changes in electrical and thermal properties of the battery pack under variable road and weather conditions, the parameters of EV should be known. The characteristics of the vehicle used in this study were selected similar to values obtained from available literature [59]. The relevant parameters are listed in Table 1 .

By selecting the driving cycle, different velocity profiles such as NEDC, HWFET, WLTP, etc. Can be used for the calculations of the characteristics of the dynamic properties of the vehicle. In this paper, WLTP driving cycle was considered and in this cycle, velocity profile, vehicle parameters and driving characteristics have great impact on power demand from battery pack. In other words, this cycle can reflect changes of battery parameters based on type of road. For the development of vehicle dynamic models, theoretical calculations were used based on the fundamental theory of vehicle dynamics [60]. The equations used in the dynamic model of the vehicle used in this study were described below. The total traction force can be calculated by using Eq. (1). Which were shown in Eq. (1) [60],

As a result, demanded power from battery pack of the EV in WLTP conditions is as shown in Fig. 1 .

From Fig. 1, instantaneous power was changed in accordance with WLTP velocity profile was reflected various acceleration zones and that was described fluctuating of electrical parameters such as current, voltage, SOC in these sections.

Developing an electrical model represents the operational behaviour of the battery pack in transient conditions was one of the main objectives of this paper. Therefore, equivalent circuit model should be constituted to estimate changes in electrical parameters such as current, voltage, SOC and etc. In the current literature, there are various approaches to build an electrical model [61–63]. A second order equivalent model (SOEC) was preferred because more detailed parameters have to be considered compared to the first ordered models [64]. The schematic diagram of the electrical model of the battery cell used in this paper is demonstrated with resistors and capacitors in Fig. 2 .

Equivalent circuit model consists of a power supply, a resistor and two series connected RC circuits. Where,
R i
is the cause of sudden increase and decrease of voltage demonstrated in Fig. 2. This resistor is the dominant parameter in the equivalent resistance of the cell [65].
R 1
-
C 1
and
R 2
-

represent reaction kinetics in electrode and diffusion process in electrolyte and active material, respectively [45]. It is stated that the diffusion process indicated by
R 2
-
C 2
affects the impedance in the lower frequency range than the charge transfer reaction represented by
R 1
-
C 1
. This means that both RC components correspond to different time dynamics. The charge transfer process occurs in a significantly shorter time scale than the diffusion process. It can be stated that the time constants are situated within different ranges, with
R 1
-
C 1
representing the fast-dynamics [66]. These parameters are especially affected by temperature, for this reason to develop more accurate model, experiments should be repeated various ambient temperature and circuit components can be calculated separately for each temperature and variable SOC [67]. The value of the parameters can be obtained from HPPC test, constant current discharge-charge method, etc. In this study, HPPC procedure was used and realized for 15–30 °C ambient temperature. The details and results of this test are mentioned in section 2.6.

The battery cell with high energy density was preferred for modelling the transient behaviour of the battery under WLTP conditions. In the current literature, research on high-capacity and also energy density cells were very limited so this study was aimed to clarify the behaviour of a cell with these characteristics. Specifications related to used battery cell was 73 Ah Lithium-ion pouch cell with NMC cathode was presented in Table 2 .

As a result of the HPPC tests was carried out at different temperatures, the values of the resistances were embedded in the model as readable table format. For all components constitute equivalent circuit, charge and discharge resistance was imported based on variable temperature and SOC. The temperature information was taken from the thermal model was mentioned in section 2.3. In this study, the initial SOC of the cell was assumed to be 100 %. Depending on drawn or given current, the change of SOC can be calculated by Equation (10) [69,70].

in Eq. (10), varies according to both temperature and current. Consequently, the capacity of the cell was determined by measuring at temperatures of 15, 20, 25 and 30 °C and currents at C/3, C/2, 1C, 1,5C and 2C-rate, with the resulting data imported into the look-up table block in Matlab/Simulink. C-rate is a measure of the rate at which a battery discharges and it is also expressed as the charge or discharge current relative to its nominal capacity. In other words, the expression 1 C-rate mentioned here refers to a discharge with a current of 73 A. Accordingly, the relevant block generates the cell capacity value depending on the temperature and instantaneous current information. In the event that the value is within the table for the given inputs, it is transferred to the output. Otherwise, interpolation or extrapolation is applied using experimental data, resulting in the generation of the

The power demanded from the battery was extracted from the electric vehicle dynamic model. The power changed between positive and negative depends on accelerating or regenerative braking. The battery encapsulated from the smallest to larger is as cell, module and pack [73–75]. Cells were electrically connected in serial or parallel for the integration of the module [76]. It provides structural support for the cells and includes thermal and electrical management units. As well known, module connected in serial constitute the battery pack [22,77]. The battery parameters as voltage and current can be defined based on battery configuration. In this study, the cell level was preferred for investigation of transient behaviour of the battery under WLTP conditions.

For the second order equivalent circuit model, the terminal voltage of the cell with known current and voltage was calculated as Equation (13) [45].

The battery parameters as resistances, current, voltage and SOC was calculated from Equations given above. The 1-D transient electrical model was developed by combining experimental tests with mathematical expressions depending on the time. These parameters were needed to estimate thermal characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The thermal model by using FDM
ce:section.ce:para: Operating parameters of the cell were affected by ambient temperature, driving conditions and its internal characteristics such as resistance, electrochemical-reactions, material type etc. [78]. Electrochemical reactions slow down, ionic conductivity decreases and the active materials in the electrolyte cannot be fully utilized at low ambient conditions, as the opposite case, the thermal runaway propagation risk occurs at high ambient conditions [47]. For this reason, the capacity and the internal resistance of the cell in terms of ambient temperature should be considered. Furthermore, these characteristics can be calculated to get the heat generation rate during transient conditions. The current applied on a cell during discharging or charging process in a driving cycle, the changes in velocity of the vehicle affects the battery temperature therefore, the variation in temperature values cannot be neglected and the temperature of the battery has to be controlled in terms of battery type [53]. For Li-Ion batteries, the ideal operating range of these batteries were ranged from 15 °C to 35 °C [79], therefore the battery thermal management systems are used to provide this range for different ambient conditions [80]. In order to design these systems effectively, it is crucial to know the heat generation and temperature variation of the cell during driving conditions [81]. For these reasons, to investigate the thermal behaviour of the battery pack and also to model the cooling plate, the transient thermal model was focused in this study and the temperature changes and heat generation rate in the battery of an EV at WLTP driving cycle were obtained.

The heat expression consists of two components, reversible and irreversible term [82,83]. Irreversible heat is expressed by the electrical properties including current, terminal voltage and open circuit voltage and it refers to ohmic resistance, polarization and side reactions heat generation. The reversible term consists of current, cell temperature and entropic term. Entropic coefficient is the rate of change of the open circuit voltage with temperature and its negative values indicate exothermic reactions while positive values represent endothermic reactions [84]. It is also calculated numerically since the heat generated and other terms are known. The expression of the heat generated in battery, named as Bernardi heat generation equation, is presented in Equation (14) [85].

). The electrical parameters of the cell are needed to create the thermal model. Therefore, in order to investigate the transient behaviour of the battery, an electrical and thermal model was interrelated.

In the current literature, the total heat generation was determined experimentally by calorimetry [86–90]. However, in this paper, the heat generated in the cell was proposed to calculate numerically based on assumptions described above. For this purpose, the finite difference method was utilized. The cell was divided into finite elements and heat generation was calculated by solving the heat transfer mechanisms for each. Discretization of the cell into surfaces shown in Fig. 3 .

In current literature, the battery cell was also modelled by using two dimensional analysis due to the thickness of the cell which was negligible compared to the other dimensions, and also the cell was assumed to be planar. Therefore, the heat conduction equation can be rearranged as stated in Equation (16) [92].

For the calculation of Eq. (16), the discretization of this equation can be performed by using different numerical methods such as finite difference, finite volume and finite elements. In this paper, the finite difference method was used. The transient model needs to know initial and boundary conditions. The battery cell was assumed in thermal equilibrium conditions and initial temperature of the cell was selected as a value of 17.5 °C by using experimental data used in this study. As for the boundary surface of the cell, heat conduction, free convection, and radiation boundary conditions are performed. The boundary condition, which considers all heat transfer mechanisms occurring on the surface of the battery cell was determined by using Eq. (17) and it was performed for the cell surface shown in Fig. 3 [91].

For calculations of generated heat by the cell, temperature data should be collected from discreatized surfaces in addition to the thermophysical properties of the cell was demonstrated in Table 3 . In other words, temperature values were obtained experimentally and also heat transfer rate was determined numerically with transient thermal model.
By using FDM, energy conservation equation can be written for each finite element by applying boundary condition as described in Table 4 .

In the equation, the convection heat transfer coefficient for each discretized surface was considered according to natural convection and it can be calculated by using Eq. (27) [91].

Consequently, the heat generated by the cell where was situated in energy conservation equation as a source term was calculated by using the transient thermal model developed in this study. ce:section.ce:section-title: The validation of the transient model
ce:section.ce:para: In the transient model, electrical equivalent circuit of the cell was developed with the data obtained from experiment, while the temperature measurements were also performed by using infrared thermal camera. Therefore, it is anticipated that the result of the model was consistent with those observed in real operating conditions. In order to validate accuracy of the transient behaviour of the cell, electrical and thermal parameters were compared with experimental data. In Fig. 4 a, the results of terminal voltage were obtained from experiment and transient model were presented as grey and black colour, respectively.

Upon analysis of the results, it is evident that the model accurately reflects the characteristic of the change in terminal voltage. Furthermore, regression analysis was conducted on the results obtained from the experimental and transient model, resulting in a R2 value of 0.99. Additionally, during the WLTP driving cycle, the model exhibited as value of about 2 % error, which was identified as the worst case scenario. Consequently, it can be concluded that the developed transient model is capable of accurately predict the voltage. Another analysed electrical parameter for validation was the SOC value. This parameter of the cell, which initially was assumed to be full, was determined to be 93.42 % at the end of the WLTP driving cycle, according to the model. In order to determine the SOC value experimentally, the current profile was applied to the cell and the OCV was measured. The OCV-SOC graph obtained as a result of the HPPC test demonstrated that the state of charge 93.0 % at end of the driving. Therefore, it can be stated that the model is highly successful in terms of estimating electrical parameters.

In addition to the validation of the model parameters, the measurement accuracy of the experimental devices was also investigated. In order to obtain accurate temperature data from the cell surface, the infrared thermal camera was calibrated by using K-type thermocouples. For this purpose, 1 C-rate constant current discharge was applied to the cell, with measurements subsequently taken from a number of points on the cell using both a thermal camera and thermocouples. Moreover, the outer surface of the lithium-ion battery used in the study was painted in a dark colour to improve the measurement accuracy and also to increase the emissivity value. Fig. 4 (b) illustrates the results of the measurement taken from the cathode region, where the highest temperature values were obtained, and grey curve represents the temperature measurement taken with the thermocouple, while the black one represents those taken with IR thermal camera. Consequently, the difference between findings was found about ±2 %

An uncertainty analysis of the experimental data collected to validate the transient model is also essential to ensure the dependability of the experiment. The uncertainty analysis was performed to observe the total experimental error by using Eq. (28) proposed by Moffat [93], where the measured parameters in the experimental studies, were the current, voltage, and temperature, were attached to the formula as value and written with Δ symbol denote the accuracy of each measurement device as reported in manufacturer's datasheet used for these purposes.

represent time interval of the measurement device and total time, respectively. The measurement devices and accuracy of each device used in the experiments, are listed in Table 5 . Accordingly, the highest uncertainty in experimental data was confirmed to be within ±1 %.

The transient electrothermal model of the battery pack was developed through the integration of experimental data, numerical, and mathematical calculations. In the context of experiments, the inputs of the model (e.g., temperature and internal resistances) were determined and the model's results were subsequently validated. The experimental setup was designed to collect voltage and temperature data from the cell under transient conditions. This system consists of climatic test chamber, DC power supply, IR thermal camera, and pouch type cell. In order to determine the electrical properties of the cell, such as internal resistance, capacity and OCV value at varying ambient temperatures, the climatic cabinet, which enables precise control of the ambient temperature, was used in this study. The Nüve ID300 climatic test chamber was employed as a thermal conditioning system, enabling the simulation of ambient temperatures within the range of 40 °C–150 °C. A 73 Ah pouch-type battery cell was positioned within the chamber. In addition, a Testo 890 IR thermal camera was used to collect temperature data from the cell surface and placed in the chamber. This device is capable of measuring temperatures between −20 °C and 100 °C, with the recorded temperature data monitored in real-time via the IRSoft control software. The ITECH 6000C bi-directional programmable DC power supply was used for the purpose of electrically conditioning the cell. This device is capable of generating an output of up to 80 V and 300 A. The power supply, which can be controlled by the software of the device, enables the cell to be tested at variable currents by importing the current profile of the driving cycle. The information about the accuracy of these devices was used in uncertainty analysis described in Table 5. In addition, experimental setup and its schematic are shown in Fig. 5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The calculation procedure of the electro-thermal model developed in this study
ce:section.ce:para: The flow chart of the calculation procedure of the electrothermal model is presented in Fig. 6 . This scheme is provided to summarize the relationship between the EV dynamics, electrical and thermal modelling blocks mentioned in the previous sections and also all input and output parameters. The calculation of the electro-thermal model was started by using the initial conditions of the EV demonstrated in Table 1. The structural, electrical and aerodynamic properties of the EV were mentioned in Table 1 which was used to calculate required power from the battery in EV dynamic model. Nevertheless, it is necessary to consider a number of additional factors in order to perform this calculation. In addition to the vehicle characteristics, it is essential to take into account the driving and ambient conditions. As this study is focused on the dynamic behaviour of the vehicle, it is assumed that the vehicle moves in accordance with the velocity profile specified in the WLTP. Accordingly, the required power described in Eq. (3) was used to calculate the power demand of the battery pack. The decision as to whether the calculated value is positive or negative determines whether the charge and discharge values of the current and resistors are used. In case this value is positive, the discharge current is determined and the discharge resistors are used. Conversely, in the otherwise scenario, the charging current is determined and the charging resistors are utilized.

HPPC test was carried out to determine the internal resistances that provided the modelling of the electrical behaviour of the cell. The heat generation during the operating of the battery and the resulting temperature changes within the cell cause the internal resistances to vary in accordance with temperature. Accordingly, in order to ensure that the model can be operated within a range of other driving cycles and in accordance with varying current profiles, the charge and discharge resistances were determined for between 15 °C and 30 °C. HPPC current profile is presented in Fig. 7 (a) . At first, discharge process is implemented at C/3 rate where 1 C-rate was corresponding to 73 A. In the initial phase of the charging process, constant current (CC) charging was performed at C/3 rate, up to a voltage of 4.2 V, which represents the upper voltage limit. Subsequently, constant voltage (CV) charging was carried out until the current dropped below 1 A. Following the completion of each sequence, the cell is rested for approximately 1 h and 30 min in order to reach a state of chemical and thermal equilibrium. After the preliminary phase, in which the cell is charged and discharged, the loop phase, consisting of current pulses, CC discharge and rest periods, is performed. A discharge pulse of 30 s, a rest period of 40 s, and a charge pulse of 10 s are applied to the cell, respectively. The value of the discharge and charge current is determined in accordance with the characteristics of the cell and the relative discharge current is 1.00, while the charge current is 0.75. Then the cell is discharged at a constant current in order to reduce its capacity by 10 % at the end of each cycle. This cycle was repeated 10 times until the cell had been completely discharged. It should be note that at the end of each cycle, the cell is allowed to rest in order to reach a steady state equilibrium. Following periods of rest, the SOC and temperature dependent OCV values were measured and entered into a look-up table as a result of the HPPC test at different temperature values. The HPPC current profile shown in Fig. 7 (a) was applied to the cell and voltage response was observed as presented in Fig. 7(b) and (c).

The sudden drop in voltage was carried out due to the dominant effect of ohmic resistance, and besides, fast and slow dynamics can be analysed in two regions, t1 and t2 as time constants, respectively. Upon analysis of Fig. 7 (b), the voltage value measured before the discharge current pulse is applied is defined as
V

. Similarly, the relevant points can be determined for the charge current pulse with the same approach. In order to identify the resistance and capacitance values that constitute the SOEC model of the cell, the change in the voltage in response to current pulses was observed. Accordingly, these elements are calculated as in Equation (29)–(33) [45].

where, the indices 0,1,2 and 3 represent the points on the voltage change curve, the values of which depend on the time constants and the indices i,1 and 2 used in the R and C terms represent the elements in the SOEC. The internal resistance of the cell during charge and discharge differs, therefore, to accurately reflect the effect of this on electrical parameters, the HPPC test utilizes the discharge current pulses to determine the discharge internal resistance (DCIR). In contrast, charge current pulses are analysed to determine the charge internal resistance (CIR). To calculate the DCIR, the discharge current pulses are analysed as illustrated in Fig. 7 (b). This involves examining the voltage change curve in response to the discharge current pulse applied in each cycle and utilizing Equation (29)–(33) to obtain the numerical value of each component. To calculate the charge internal resistance (CIR), the voltage change curve in response to the charge current pulse applied in each cycle analysed. The numerical value of each component is obtained by substituting the values with subscript “chg” derived from Fig. 7 (c) into Equations (29)–(33). The calculations were carried out for each resistance and capacitance component, separately for each 10 % decrease of SOC at 15, 20, 25 and 30 °C ambient temperature for charge and discharge, with the results imported to the model as look-up table. Nevertheless, in order to accurately predict the behaviour of the cell under dynamic conditions, it is necessary to determine the electrical parameters corresponding to intermediate values of temperature and SOC. The Matlab/Simulink platform's look-up table block, which imports data, outputs the corresponding output value for a given input value of SOC and temperature if the data is present in the table of values. Otherwise, the block generates the output value through interpolation. In other words, this block calculates the electrical parameters corresponding to intermediate values of temperature and SOC through a curve fitting process using experimental data. The internal resistances of the cell are mentioned in results section.

The open circuit voltage and internal resistance values can be determined based on the initial and instantaneous value of the SOC and temperature of the cell. The surface temperature of the cell (T
b ) is used to denote the average temperature of the cell surface. The cell surface was discretized into a number of equivalent surfaces as in the FDM method shown in Fig. 3 and real-time temperature data was collected from the midpoints of these surfaces using IR thermal camera. Subsequently, the average temperature for the cell, which is assumed to be planar due to the thickness of the cell which was negligible compared to the other dimensions, was calculated by area-weighted average value as expressed in Eq. (34), where T
i and A
i were the surface temperature measured at the centroid of each element and area of each element, respectively [94]. A
t was the total surface area of the battery cell used in this study and n is the number of equivalent surfaces which was selected as 45 in this study.

As a result of determination of the required power and open circuit voltage of the battery pack and depending on the pack and module configuration, the current drawn from the cell was calculated as described in Equation (11) and (12). The terminal voltage of the cell whose current, internal resistances and open circuit voltage are known is calculated as is Equation (13).

In the developed model, the calculated electrical parameters are employed as input for the calculation of the parameters of the thermal model. The temperature data collected from the battery cell surface is used in thermal model calculations. This data is obtained as a result of the experimental studies. The finite difference method was used to calculate the heat generated on the discrete surfaces in the cell. For each discrete surface, the heat generated is calculated using the thermophysical properties of the cell mentioned in Table 3 and the heat transfer equations expressed in Table 4, where the boundary condition considering all heat transfer mechanisms is applied at each boundary element. The calculated heat generation is comprised of two terms as expressed in Equation (14) known as reversible and irreversible. In the calculation of the irreversible term, parameters from the electrical model are employed, whereas in the calculation of the reversible term, current, cell temperature and entropic term values are utilized. Subsequently, the entropic term can be calculated using the values of the heat generation and electrical parameters that have been previously determined. In summary, the inputs to this model were parameters of the EV presented in Table 1, the battery pack configuration, the cell thermophysical properties presented in Table 3, the velocity profile of the driving cycle, the initial SOC value, the OCV and the internal resistances of charge and discharge as a result of the HPPC test depending on the temperature and SOC, the cell capacity value obtained depending on temperature and current and transferred via look-up table, and real-time temperature data. The outputs of the model were the required power from battery pack, OCV, terminal voltage, cell current, equivalent internal resistance, SOC, heat generation rate, reversible heat, irreversible heat and entropic term. The cycle time is then evaluated. If it exceeds 1800 s, which is the WLTP cycle time, the model terminates the calculation. Conversely, if the cycle time is less than this threshold, the calculation continues with the feedback. Accordingly, the cell capacity is determined through a look-up table, according to the temperature and current information, and the instantaneous SOC value is calculated by determining the consumed SOC. Furthermore, real-time temperature data is collected from the cell and fed back. The loop then starts again with the calculation of the required power from battery pack.

This section presents the comparative results between experimental data and calculated model parameters which were developed to investigate electrothermal transient behaviour of 73 Ah pouch used in EV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The calculated electrical parameters by using electrical model
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrical model has been developed with the objective of accurately reflecting the variation in electrical parameters of a vehicle during its operation within the WLTP cycle under dynamic conditions. Therefore, HPPC test was conducted to constitute the SOEC of the cell and the internal resistances were calculated for varying temperature and SOC. In Fig. 8 (a) , the change of equivalent internal resistance (EIR) of the cell depending on SOC and temperature (15, 20, 25 and 30 °C) is presented. The greatest resistances were found at SOC values close to the point of discharge of the cell for all temperatures investigated. It was observed that the EIR exhibited an increase in accordance with the decrease in temperature, although the effect was more pronounced at low values of the SOC. The reason for this phenomenon can be explained by the strong influence of temperature on the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte and electrodes, as well as the rate of chemical reaction within the cell. Furthermore, at a given temperature, there is a discrepancy of approximately 2 times between the maximum and minimum values of EIR as a result of the change in SOC. It should be noted that in this study, SOC values below 5 % were not included within the calculations. In accordance with the findings, for each of the temperatures under consideration, the maximum EIR was recorded at 5 % SOC, while the minimum EIR was noted at 50 % SOC. As it is known, the cell equivalent circuit consists of resistor and series connected RC circuits. As can be seen, the ohmic resistance denotes as
R i
has a much higher value than other resistance terms. Upon examination of the resistance values obtained at 30 °C, it becomes evident that the equivalent resistance value and ohmic resistance value at this temperature are nearly identical. As a result, the ohmic resistance is the most significant term to describe the behaviour of the cell. As it is known, this term represents sudden increases and decreases in voltage. Fig. 8 (c) presents the variation of the EIR of an EV under WLTP driving conditions. Accordingly, the battery is discharged and charged due to accelerations and decelerations during this cycle. Given that the charge and discharge resistance of a cell are distinct, the EIR undergoes a transition between the values of these two states during the cycle. However, the EIR is reduced as a consequence of the decline in SOC and the rise in temperature during the operation of the cell. This effect becomes more visible after 1000 s, as the highest current is drawn in the final stages of the cycle.

One of the main objective of this study was to accurately estimate the voltage variation under dynamic conditions. To ensure this, it is essential to accurately analyse the thermal and electrical changes occurring within the cell during its operation. Fig. 4 (a) presents the voltage change observed in a single battery cell. As it is known, at the beginning of the study, it was assumed that the cell was full. It is evident that the voltage varies in proportion to the power demanded from the battery, which is calculated in accordance with the road conditions, velocity profile, vehicle characteristics and battery pack configuration. Furthermore, an experiment was conducted to verify the voltage profile obtained from the model, with the results presented and compared in the relevant graph. The results indicate that the developed model is capable of accurately establishing the voltage variation of an EV at WLTP driving cycle, with a worst-case error of 2 %. Furthermore, the R2 value was calculated to be 0.99 in the regression analysis performed to evaluate the correlation between the experimental and calculated results. As a result, it is found that the developed transient model demonstrates a good agreement with the experimental data, providing an accurate representation of the behaviour of the battery cell under dynamic conditions.

Another electrical parameter that was analysed was the state of charge. The variation of the instantaneous SOC value of the battery cell during the WLTP cycle is presented in Fig. 8 (d). In order to determine the instantaneous SOC value of the cell, the SOC-OCV diagram generated from the HPPC test was employed. The open circuit voltage parameter was obtained for the specified temperature and SOC values and imported into the model as readable table format. The intermediate values were obtained by performing curve fitting in conditions that differed from the detected points. Accordingly, the cell was initiated with the assumption that the cell was fully charged, or 100 % SOC. At the end of the WLTP driving cycle, SOC was recorded as 93.42 %. The graph indicates that a notable change in SOC value occurs towards the end of the cycle, when the current is sustained at high levels. It was also stated that the cell capacity was taken as a variable depending on the current and temperature data during estimation of SOC. Accordingly, the effect of the capacity change on SOC was limited due to the low increase in cell temperature, as mentioned in section 3.2, and the low levels of WLTP current in general as seen in Fig. 8 (d). However, at higher current levels, varying driving cycles and repeated cycles, it is anticipated that the impact of capacity change will become increasingly evident.

In the electrical model, the OCV is initially identified and subsequently the SOC value is determined in accordance with this voltage. An experimental study was conducted to evaluate the model's accuracy in this regard. The study found that the SOC value reached 93 % at the end of the cycle. Therefore, it is understood that the SOC value can be estimated with a high degree of consistency using this model.

The current profile of the cell at WLTP cycle is also presented in Fig. 8 (d). The profile is the calculated according to the voltage and power demanded by the battery. The current exhibited oscillatory behaviour in accordance with the velocity profile, with the maximum value recorded at the end of the cycle. This value corresponds to approximately 1 C-rate. Nevertheless, in general, it is understood that the current drawn from the battery for the WLTP cycle is at low levels. Consequently, the alteration in electrical and thermal characteristics observed in the sequence representing the urban driving is relatively minor. It should be noted that the current calculation for a cell in an electrical model is vehicle specific. This is because the electrical connection configurations at the pack and module levels are crucial determinants of the calculation.

Consequently, the electrical outputs, including current, voltage, state of charge and equivalent internal resistance, can be obtained with a high degree of consistency when using transient electrical model that incorporates the thermal factors of the cell whose internal properties defining by the experimental studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The temperature data obtained from experimental study and the calculated heat generation rate by using electro-thermal model during WLTP cycle
ce:section.ce:para: One of the primary objectives of this study is to demonstrate the transient thermal behaviour of the battery in a consistent manner. As is well-known, studies with high energy density cells are limited in the literature. At the same time, this study also aims to explain the behaviour of pouch cells with polarity opposite to each other.

The average temperature of the cell of an EV under WLTP conditions is presented in Fig. 9 (a) , while the thermal image of the cell at the beginning and end of the cycle are shown in Fig. 9(b) and (c), respectively. In order to determine the average temperature of the cell, the area-weighted average of the real-time temperature data collected from the cell surface was calculated as mentioned in section 2.6.

Before the WLTP current was applied to the cell, it was subjected to a process of thermal equilibrium, during which its initial temperature was recorded as 17.5 °C, which is also the ambient temperature. It is understood that the average cell temperature is correlated with the change in current. The temperature reached its highest point at the end of the cycle, in accordance with the increase in current drawn from the battery. However, at low current values, the temperature change was not significantly affected. Furthermore, it was observed that the oscillations in the current had a minimal effect on the temperature, and that the continuity and magnitude of the value of the current were primary causes of the observed increase in temperature. The results indicate that the average temperature of the cell, which was 17.5 °C at the beginning of the experiment, reached a maximum value of 18.3 °C at the end of the cycle. It can be observed that the average temperature increases by a 0.8 °C under WLTP driving conditions. Nevertheless, the outcome of this experiment may vary depending on the SOC and thermal conditions. For instance, it is clear that when the SOC value is low, the equivalent internal resistance will increase significantly, which will consequently result in increase in temperature. These calculations can be easily repeated of a vehicle with higher weight, in this case, the higher temperature increase and also lower SOC can be achieved under WLTP conditions.

The temperature measurement by infrared thermal camera used in the experiments were demonstrated in Fig. 9 (b) and (c) indicate that the highest temperature value was recorded at cathode at the end of the cycle. The electrolyte resistance represents the largest contribution to the heat generated. Given that a greater loss of electrolyte occurs at the cathode than at the anode, it follows that a greater loss of energy will occur at the cathode due to the electrolyte heat term [95]. This will result in a higher temperature at the cathode, in accordance with the experimental findings.

As it is known, heat generation occurs during the operation of the cell. In this paper, this parameter is obtained by the finite difference method, utilizing temperature data. Thus, by combining experimental and numerical studies, a transient model that calculates the heat generation at points on the cell surface is developed. By dividing the cell surface into a finite number of elements and solving the conservation of energy equation by applying boundary conditions on these surfaces, it should be possible to calculate point heat generation. The greatest temperature change is observed in the cathode under WLTP driving conditions. Consequently, Fig. 9 (d) presents the graph of the change in heat generation in the cathode.

Heat generation is influenced by a number of factors, including electrical parameters, cell temperature and the entropic term. It can be observed that there are sudden increases and decreases in heat generation due to fluctuations in current and temperature. Accordingly, this term can reach a peak value of ±1.75 105 Wm−3 under WLTP conditions at the cathode point. The average heat generation on the cell surface was calculated as 0.32 W by taking the area-weighted average of the heat generation in each finite element on the surface. The main reason for this value being at these levels is that the temperature rise at WLTP is only 0.8 °C. In order to confirm the accuracy of the model, the cell was discharged at 1 C-rate and its thermal behaviour was investigated. Consequently, the average heat generation during the discharge of the cell at 73 A constant current was calculated to be 6 W. This result is consistent with the results obtained with similar cells in the literature [96–98]. Thus, the point heat generation of the cell is calculated with the model developed by combining experimental and numerical studies without using a calorimeter.

The variation of entropic term during the WLTP driving cycle is presented in Fig. 9 (e). It fluctuated during the WLTP driving cycle and reached a maximum value of 0.2 and minimum value of −0.2. As it well-known, this term is highly dependent on the SOC value, with a tendency to deviate from zero at low SOC values. However, in the examined conditions, the SOC only dropped to 93 % and this occurred at end of the time period. The abrupt fluctuations in the entropic term were also observed in the last part of the cycle, occurring in parallel with this phenomenon. As a result, the entropic term, which is a time-consuming process to obtain experimentally, is calculated numerically based on heat generation with the developed transient model.

Consequently, the results of 1D dynamic model of a pouch cell under dynamic conditions were acceptable considering vehicle properties and all assumptions used in this paper. By using the predicted heat generation rate under WLTP driving cycle, an effective cooling plate for battery pack level can also be designed for thermal management system of EVs [99].

This paper proposes a novel transient model to estimate the electrical and thermal behaviour of a 73 Ah Li-ion pouch cell of an EV under WLTP driving conditions. For this purpose, a detailed transient model was developed through the integration of experimental data with numerical and theoretical calculations, thereby ensuring the consistency of the model results. It was shown that the results of model had well compromised with the theoretical and experimental data presented in this study. With the considerations given above, the conclusions can be stated as follows.
•
The results of the proposed electrothermal model demonstrated that the terminal voltage of the battery cell can be predicted with maximum relative error of 2.0 % and R2 value of 0.99.
•
The change in SOC parameter during the WLTP driving cycle can be predicted with a relative error of 0.45 % by using the developed model. Accordingly, SOC value decreased by 7 % for given vehicle characteristics in the WLTP driving cycle.
•
With the proposed model, the heat generation rate and also entropic term can be estimated numerically without using a calorimeter. Accordingly, heat generated by the battery cell is acquired as 0.32 W under WLTP driving conditions and also 6 W at 1 C-rate constant current by using finite difference method numerically.
•
This study contributed to finding out the electrical and thermal properties of high energy density pouch cells with opposite polarity terminals due to limited studies available in the literature.

As a result, the estimated heat generation rate under WLTP driving cycle can be used to design an effective cooling channel geometry for thermal management of a high energy density Lithium-ion pouch battery cell. In future studies, the effect of housing elements, plates and compression foam and electrical components on the thermal and electrical properties of a high energy density pouch cell under different driving conditions will be investigated by developing a full battery model.
Berkay Tahirağaoğlu: Software, Writing – original draft. Gökhan Sevilgen: Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Halil Sadettin Hamut: Writing – review & editing.
